threats.title: Genesis Deposit Retroactive Reward Theft via Missing User Checkpoint in depositsForGenesis
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2._depositsForGenesis calls updateReward(address(0)) instead of updateReward(_tokenOwner). This skips setting userRewardPerTokenPaid[_tokenOwner], leaving it at 0. When the genesis depositor later calls getReward() or earned(), they receive rewards retroactively from the beginning of the reward period — even for time before their deposit — stealing from existing stakers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The modifier updateReward(address(0)) explicitly skips user-level checkpoint updates. Any user deposited via genesis who has userRewardPerTokenPaid=0 and positive _balanceOf will claim earned = balance * rewardPerTokenStored / 1e18, which includes all historically accumulated rewards.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateReward(address(0)) modifier on _depositsForGenesis skips setting userRewardPerTokenPaid[_tokenOwner]; _balances[_tokenOwner] not updated but _totalSupply is incremented

preconditions: Gauge has been receiving rewards via notifyRewardAmount (rewardPerTokenStored > 0)
genesisPool is set and calls depositsForGenesis
The deposited user had no prior interaction with the gauge (userRewardPerTokenPaid = 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: depositsForGenesis uses updateReward(address(0)) while _deposit uses updateReward(account) — inconsistent checkpoint behavior
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires genesisPool to be configured and to initiate deposit, but the reward theft is automatic once deposit occurs
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — any time rewards accumulated before genesis deposit, the retroactive claim is available

operational_questions: Can notifyRewardAmount be called before depositsForGenesis for the same gauge?
What is the typical rewardPerTokenStored at the time genesis deposits happen?
How many reward tokens can be stolen per genesis depositor?
validation_requirements: Deploy gauge, distribute rewards, then call depositsForGenesis — verify user can claim retroactive rewards
Calculate maximum theft = genesisBalance * rewardPerTokenStored / 1e18

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Gauge receives rewards via notifyRewardAmount, accumulating rewardPerTokenStored over time
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Genesis pool deposits LP tokens for a user via depositsForGenesis — userRewardPerTokenPaid stays 0
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User calls getReward() — earned() returns balance * (rewardPerTokenStored - 0) / 1e18
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User receives rewards proportional to entire accumulated history, draining reward pool from legitimate stakers
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: No special coordination needed — occurs naturally in genesis launch flow

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Reward token insolvency for the gauge
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Legitimate stakers receive reduced or zero rewards
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires new reward injection or gauge redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reward distribution for gauges with genesis deposits

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None — exploitable by any genesis depositor
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low — happens automatically during genesis launch
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Basic — just call getReward() after genesis deposit
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after genesis deposit when rewards have accumulated

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large getReward claims from newly deposited genesis users
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Reward token balance in gauge dropping faster than expected
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Genesis depositors claiming rewards immediately after deposit

threats.title: FoT Swap Router Uses Wrong Pool for Amount Calculation Causing Reverts or Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2._swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens calls getAmountOut(amountInput, input, output) which returns the BEST output across BOTH stable and volatile pools. However, the actual swap is executed on the specific pool determined by routes[i].stable. When the best pool differs from the routed pool, the calculated amountOutput is incorrect — causing K invariant failures (revert) or suboptimal execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: getAmountOut selects the higher of amountStable/amountVolatile. If stable gives higher output but route specifies volatile, swap uses inflated amount on wrong pool. The pair's K check will fail if the amount exceeds what the volatile pool can produce.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens calls getAmountOut which picks best pool, but pair.swap executes on pairFor(routes[i].from, routes[i].to, routes[i].stable) — pool type mismatch

preconditions: Both stable and volatile pools exist for the same token pair
The two pools have different liquidity profiles yielding different amounts
User uses FoT-supporting swap functions

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: getAmountOut returns (amount, stable) but _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens only uses amount, ignoring which pool was selected
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — affects all FoT token swaps

operational_questions: How many token pairs have both stable and volatile pools?
What is the typical output divergence between stable and volatile pools?
validation_requirements: Test FoT swap where stable pool gives higher output but route specifies volatile — verify revert
Test reverse case where volatile gives higher — verify user receives less than expected

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify token pair with both stable and volatile pools where outputs differ significantly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: getAmountOut returns stable pool's higher amount; swap on volatile pool fails K check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All FoT swaps on this pair revert, effectively DoSing FoT token trading

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All fee-on-transfer token swaps may revert or produce incorrect outputs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires router redeployment with corrected logic

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: None — any user calling FoT swap functions
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Anytime both stable and volatile pools exist for a pair

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to swap FoT tokens through the router
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Consistent swap failures on specific token pairs

threats.title: swapExactTokensForTokensSimple Completely Non-Functional Due to Uninitialized Route Fields
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2.swapExactTokensForTokensSimple creates a route[] but only sets from, to, stable, and concentrated fields. The pair and receiver fields default to address(0). For non-concentrated swaps, getAmountsOut checks isPair(address(0))=false, returning amounts[1]=0, causing amountOutMin check failure. For concentrated swaps, pairToDeployer(address(0)) returns address(0), causing quoter failure. Even if bypassed, _swap sends output to receiver=address(0), permanently burning tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The route struct has 6 fields but only 4 are set. Missing pair causes getAmountsOut to return 0. Missing receiver causes _swap to send tokens to address(0).

proof_of_signal.pattern: routes[0].pair and routes[0].receiver never assigned; default to address(0) in memory allocation

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with any parameters

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Route struct fields pair and receiver are never set but are used in getAmountsOut and _swap

operational_questions: Is this function called by any frontend or integration?
Are users encountering reverts when using this function?
validation_requirements: Call swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with valid parameters — verify it always reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: getAmountsOut returns 0 for non-concentrated (isPair(address(0))=false), or fails for concentrated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts, user loses gas fees
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Function is completely non-functional as a core protocol entry point

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Core swap function non-functional; users lose gas

threats.title: RouterV2._swap Ignores _to Parameter — swapExactTokensForETH Breaks if Receiver Misconfigured
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2._swap sends swap outputs to routes[i].receiver, completely ignoring the _to parameter. In swapExactTokensForETH, the router passes _to=address(this) expecting to receive WETH for unwrapping, but if the last route's receiver is not address(this), WETH goes elsewhere. The router then calls wETH.withdraw() on a balance it doesn't have, causing revert or the user losing their WETH output.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _to parameter is dead code in _swap. All output routing depends solely on routes[i].receiver. Public swap functions pass _to but it has no effect on token flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _swap function parameter _to is never referenced in function body; routes[i].receiver determines all output destinations

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForETH (or similar) with routes where last receiver != expected destination
For swapExactTokensForETH: last route's receiver must be address(router) for WETH unwrap to work

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: swapExactTokensForETH passes address(this) as _to but _swap never uses it
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time the routes[].receiver fields don't match the expected _to destination

operational_questions: Do frontends correctly set routes[i].receiver for each swap type?
Have any swapExactTokensForETH calls failed because WETH didn't arrive at router?
validation_requirements: Call swapExactTokensForETH with last route receiver != router address — verify revert on wETH.withdraw
Verify _to parameter has zero effect on _swap execution

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User constructs route for swapExactTokensForETH with receiver set to user address (intuitive but wrong)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: WETH goes to user's address instead of router
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Router calls wETH.withdraw on zero balance — reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User loses gas; if receiver is address(0), output tokens are burned
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — triggered by normal usage with incorrect route construction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: ETH swap functions may fail or send tokens to wrong addresses
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: swapExactTokensForETH, all multi-hop swaps relying on _to parameter

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: WETH stuck in user addresses instead of being unwrapped

threats.title: GlobalRouter.exactInput Is a No-Op That Silently Returns Zero and Traps ETH
threats.hypothesis_description: GlobalRouter.exactInput has its entire function body commented out, returning amountOut=0 silently. The function is marked payable, so any ETH sent with the call is permanently locked in the GlobalRouter contract which has no withdrawal mechanism. ERC20 calls would silently return 0 output without moving any tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function body contains only commented-out code. The payable modifier allows ETH to be received. GlobalRouter has no receive/fallback (except via payable functions) and no ETH withdrawal function.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function body is entirely commented out between /* and */; returns default uint256(0) for amountOut; payable modifier accepts ETH

preconditions: User or integrator calls GlobalRouter.exactInput
For ETH loss: user sends msg.value > 0 with the call

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Entire function is dead code wrapped in comments; payable allows ETH receipt with no processing
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — no fix path without redeployment

operational_questions: Is GlobalRouter.exactInput referenced by any frontend or integration?
Is there any ETH balance stuck in the GlobalRouter?
validation_requirements: Call exactInput with ETH value — verify ETH is permanently stuck
Verify no withdrawal function exists on GlobalRouter

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User calls exactInput expecting V3-style multi-hop swap
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Any ETH sent is permanently locked in GlobalRouter

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: V3-style swaps via GlobalRouter completely non-functional; ETH permanently lost

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Setting Can DoS All Swaps via Arithmetic Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no upper bound validation. If MAX_REFERRAL_FEE or a custom referral fee is set > 10000, then in Pair._update0/_update1, `_referralFee = amount * _maxRef / 10000` exceeds `amount`, causing `amount -= _referralFee` to underflow and revert (Solidity 0.8.13). This permanently DoSes all swaps on affected pairs.
threats.investigation_rationale: setReferralFee has zero validation (unlike setFee which checks <= MAX_FEE). A value of 10001 makes _referralFee > amount, causing underflow revert in _update0/_update1 which is called from every swap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setReferralFee(uint256 _refFee) has no require(_refFee <= 10000) or similar bound; compare to setFee which has require(_fee <= MAX_FEE)

preconditions: feeManager calls setReferralFee with value > 10000 (accidentally or maliciously)
dibs address is set (non-zero) so referral fee logic is active
Any swap occurs on any pair

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setReferralFee has no bounds check while setFee does; asymmetric validation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: All swaps DoS'd from the moment the value is set

operational_questions: What is the current MAX_REFERRAL_FEE value on-chain?
Are there any custom referral fees set for specific pairs?
Could a feeManager accidentally set an invalid value?
validation_requirements: Set MAX_REFERRAL_FEE to 10001 — verify all swaps revert
Verify no upper bound check exists in either setReferralFee or setCustomReferralFee

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: feeManager calls setReferralFee(10001) — either accidentally or via compromised key
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All swaps on all pairs with dibs != address(0) are permanently DoSed

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All swap operations DoSed on all pairs
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: feeManager must call setReferralFee with valid value; if feeManager key is lost, protocol is permanently DoSed
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All swap operations across the protocol

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sudden swap revert rate increase to 100%

threats.title: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn Infinite Loop Due to Unsigned Integer Underflow
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper.getAmountsIn uses `for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--)`. Since `i` is uint256, when it reaches 0 and decrements, it wraps to type(uint256).max. The condition `i >= 0` is always true for unsigned integers, creating an infinite loop that exhausts all gas.
threats.investigation_rationale: In Solidity 0.8.13, uint256 cannot be negative. The decrement from 0 causes underflow which in unchecked blocks wraps, but since this isn't in an unchecked block, it will revert with arithmetic underflow after processing i=0. Actually, since Solidity 0.8 reverts on underflow, the function will process i=0 correctly then revert on i-- when i=0.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint i = routes.length-1; i >= 0; i--) — uint underflow on i-- when i==0 causes revert

preconditions: Any call to TradeHelper.getAmountsIn with any valid routes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Reverse iteration loop with unsigned counter and >= 0 termination condition
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None — any caller triggers the bug

operational_questions: Is getAmountsIn used by any frontend, integration, or on-chain contract?
Does GlobalRouter or any other contract depend on this function?
validation_requirements: Call getAmountsIn with a single-route array — verify it reverts with arithmetic underflow

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Call getAmountsIn with any valid route
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Loop processes i=0, then i-- causes uint underflow revert in Solidity 0.8
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Any integrator relying on reverse amount calculations is broken

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reverse amount calculation unavailable; integrators using this function are broken

threats.title: TradeHelper.getAmountInStable Returns Incorrect Values Due to Inverted Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: TradeHelper.getAmountInStable computes y_1 = reserveOut + output instead of reserveOut - output. When calculating the input required for a desired output, it should model removing tokens from the output reserve, not adding to it. This inverts the calculation direction, returning completely wrong amountIn values. Additionally, the final conversion uses decimalsOut instead of decimalsIn.
threats.investigation_rationale: The stable AMM uses x³y + xy³ = k. To find input for desired output, the new output reserve should be reserveOut - output (tokens leaving the pool), but the code adds output to reserveOut.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint y_1 = reserveOut + output; should be reserveOut - output; also amountInNoFees uses decimalsOut instead of decimalsIn at line 110

preconditions: Any call to getAmountInStable for a stable pair

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Adding output to reserveOut models the opposite direction — injecting tokens instead of removing them
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — returns wrong values for all stable pool reverse quotes

operational_questions: Are any on-chain contracts or frontends using getAmountInStable for trade execution?
Could incorrect quotes lead to unfavorable trades if used for slippage calculations?
validation_requirements: Compare getAmountInStable output against actual swap requirements for a stable pair
Verify the calculation error produces significantly wrong results

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Frontend or integrator calls getAmountInStable to quote required input for desired output
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Function returns incorrect amountIn (wrong direction + wrong decimal conversion)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: User executes trade with wrong input amount, receiving less output than expected
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Systematic mispricing of all stable pool reverse quotes
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None — view function returns wrong values

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect quotes for stable pool reverse calculations
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All stable pool reverse amount calculations

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users getting unexpected swap results when using TradeHelper quotes
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: getAmountInStable results inconsistent with forward getAmountOut

threats.title: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe Tautological Zero-Address Check Allows Breaking Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe has require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA') which is always true since all addresses are >= address(0) as uint160. This allows setting internal_bribe to address(0). When claimFees subsequently calls IERC20.safeApprove(address(0), ...) and IBribe(address(0)).notifyRewardAmount(...), these calls fail, permanently breaking LP fee forwarding to the bribe system.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comparison >= for addresses always evaluates true. This is a logic error — it should be require(_int != address(0), 'ZA'). Setting internal_bribe to address(0) breaks claimFees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA') — always true; should be require(_int != address(0))

preconditions: Owner or GAUGE_ADMIN calls setInternalBribe(address(0)) via direct call or GaugeFactory.setInternalBribe
isForPair is true (gauge is for a pair)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Tautological validation allows any address including zero; claimFees then calls approve/notifyRewardAmount on address(0)
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires owner or admin to call setInternalBribe with address(0)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent once set — claimFees reverts until internal_bribe is fixed

operational_questions: Has setInternalBribe been called with address(0) on any gauge?
Are there automated calls to claimFees that would be affected?
validation_requirements: Call setInternalBribe(address(0)) — verify it succeeds
Call claimFees afterward — verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner or admin accidentally calls setInternalBribe(address(0))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: All subsequent claimFees calls revert — LP fee distribution permanently broken

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: LP fee distribution to bribe system halted for affected gauge
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low — owner calls setInternalBribe with correct address

threats.title: Pair Mint and Burn Not Subject to Pause Check — Stale Price Exploitation During Emergency Pause
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.swap checks PairFactory.isPaused but Pair.mint and Pair.burn do not. During a pause (intended for emergencies), attackers can add heavily one-sided liquidity at stale prices. When the pause is lifted and trading resumes, the attacker can immediately swap against their skewed liquidity to extract value from the pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: swap has require(!PairFactory(factory).isPaused()) but mint and burn have no such check. This asymmetry allows liquidity manipulation during what should be a frozen state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap checks isPaused at line 268; mint (line 241) and burn (line 255) have no isPaused check

preconditions: Protocol pauses swaps via PairFactory.setPause(true)
External price moves significantly during pause period
Attacker adds one-sided liquidity during pause

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Asymmetric pause enforcement — swaps blocked but mint/burn allowed during emergency
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires protocol to activate pause (legitimate emergency scenario)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During any pause period where external prices diverge from pool reserves

operational_questions: How long are pauses typically active?
What price movement could occur during a typical pause window?
Is there a monitoring system to detect liquidity manipulation during pause?
validation_requirements: Simulate: pause swaps, add one-sided liquidity, unpause, swap to extract value
Calculate profit vs cost of providing one-sided liquidity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol pauses swaps due to emergency (legitimate operational action)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: External prices diverge from pool reserves during pause
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker adds heavily one-sided liquidity at stale prices via mint
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: On unpause, attacker swaps against skewed liquidity, extracting value from existing LPs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Must monitor for pause events and act during pause window

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: LP value extraction during emergency events
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium — damage already done once unpause occurs

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None — requires capital for liquidity provision
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Medium — must act during pause window
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Medium — requires understanding of AMM mechanics

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Large one-sided liquidity additions during pauses
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Immediate swaps after unpause from the same addresses

threats.title: RouterV2 Owner Has No Transfer Mechanism — Permanent Single-Key Control Over Critical Configuration
threats.hypothesis_description: RouterV2 uses a simple `owner` variable set in the constructor with no transfer or renounce function. If the owner key is compromised, the attacker can permanently change swapRouter to a malicious contract that steals all tokens approved to the router during concentrated liquidity swaps. If the key is lost, critical parameters can never be updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: RouterV2 doesn't inherit OZ Ownable; owner is set once in constructor. setSwapRouter, setAlgebraFactory, setQuoterV2, setAlgebraPoolAPI are all gated by require(msg.sender == owner) with no mechanism to change owner.
threats.config_evidence: V1/DeployRouterV2.js shows RouterV2 deployed with constructor args; no post-deploy ownership transfer. Owner is the deployer EOA.

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership or renounceOwnership function anywhere in contract

preconditions: Owner private key is compromised or lost

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: No ownership transfer mechanism; permanent single-point-of-failure for router configuration
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Single EOA controls all router configuration permanently
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent — if compromised, malicious swapRouter steals approved tokens

operational_questions: Is the router owner a multisig or an EOA?
How many tokens are currently approved to the router for concentrated swaps?
Can the router be replaced without disrupting users?
validation_requirements: Verify no ownership transfer function exists in RouterV2
Check on-chain what address is the current owner

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker compromises router owner private key
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Next concentrated liquidity swap approves tokens to malicious contract

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Full control over router's external dependencies
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Router must be redeployed; all user approvals must be revoked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: swapRouter address changing to unknown contract
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Unexpected setter function calls from owner
